findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected without validation in text/javascript response - XSS via arbitrary JavaScript injection
findings.code_snippet: String content = callback == null ? json : callback + "(" + json + ");";
findings.reasoning: The JSONP `callback` parameter (from `pReq.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue())`) is retrieved at line 472 directly from the HTTP request without any sanitization or validation. It is then concatenated directly into the response body at line 499 as `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. The content type for JSONP responses is set to `text/javascript` (line 473). Since there is no alphanumeric-only validation or any other sanitization of the callback name, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by providing a malicious callback value like `alert(document.cookie)//`. When a victim's browser loads this response, the injected code will execute. This is a classic JSONP callback injection vulnerability.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request parameter 'callback' -> pReq.getParameter() -> callback variable -> string concatenation -> response body with text/javascript content type
evidence.notes: The ConfigKey.CALLBACK is defined as CALLBACK('callback', false, true) with no validation constraints. The getMimeType() method also allows the MIME type to be controlled by user request parameter, defaulting to text/plain.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with a malicious JSONP callback parameter that executes JavaScript when victim visits the link in a browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must be authenticated to the Jolokia endpoint or the endpoint must be publicly accessible; attacker tricks victim into clicking a crafted URL
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site scripting (XSS) via JSONP callback injection

flags.message: Classic JSONP callback XSS vulnerability - unvalidated user input reflected in text/javascript response
flags.evidence: Line 472: callback retrieved directly from HTTP request parameter. Line 499: callback concatenated without sanitization into response with text/javascript content type. No validation present anywhere in codebase per evidence.

sanity_check.reasoning: The vulnerability is definitively confirmed by the code: the JSONP callback parameter is taken directly from the HTTP request at line 472 without any sanitization or validation, and then concatenated into the response body at line 499 with a content type of text/javascript. This is a textbook JSONP XSS vulnerability - an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by providing a malicious callback value such as 'alert(document.cookie)//' which would execute in the victim's browser. This is production code in a web middleware/monitoring library (Jolokia), making it directly exploitable by any user who can reach the HTTP endpoint.

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter written directly to streaming response without validation - XSS via arbitrary JavaScript injection
findings.reasoning: In the streaming path of the JSONP response, `IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose()` at line 36 writes the `callback` string directly to the response writer without any sanitization or validation. The callback parameter originates from `pReq.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue())` in `AgentServlet.sendStreamingResponse()` and is passed as-is to this method. Since no alphanumeric-only or any other validation is applied, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript. The content type is set to `text/javascript` before this write occurs, making the injected code executable in the browser.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request parameter 'callback' -> pReq.getParameter() -> callback variable -> sendStreamingResponse() -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() -> pWriter.write(callback) -> response body with text/javascript content type
evidence.notes: This is the streaming variant of the same vulnerability found in sendAllJSON(). Both paths write the callback without validation.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with a malicious JSONP callback parameter that executes JavaScript when victim visits the link in a browser via the streaming response path
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must be authenticated to the Jolokia endpoint or the endpoint must be publicly accessible; attacker tricks victim into clicking a crafted URL
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site scripting (XSS) via JSONP callback injection in streaming response

flags.message: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter enables XSS via arbitrary JavaScript injection
flags.evidence: The callback string from HTTP request parameter is written directly to the response via pWriter.write(callback) without any alphanumeric validation or sanitization. Content-type is text/javascript, making injected scripts executable.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly shows the callback parameter being written directly to the response writer without any validation or sanitization. The data flow from HTTP request parameter through to response output is confirmed in both the code snippet and evidence. This is a well-known JSONP XSS vulnerability pattern where an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious callback parameter value (e.g., 'alert(1)//'). Since this is a web/monitoring agent that handles HTTP requests, the vulnerability is directly exploitable by any client making requests to the Jolokia agent endpoint.

findings.description: CodeQL flagged LogHandler.java stdout logging as XSS sink - false positive, output goes to System.out not HTTP response
findings.reasoning: The CodeQL finding identifies LogHandler.java line 82 as the XSS sink, but this is a false positive. The `StdoutLogHandler.log()` method writes to `System.out` (stdout), not to an HTTP response. Even though user-controlled data (request URI/parameters) flows into this method via the debug logging chain (AgentServlet -> HttpRequestHandler -> logHandler.debug()), the data is written to standard output of the server process, not into an HTML or JavaScript response that could be rendered by a browser. The actual XSS vulnerability exists in the JSONP callback handling in AgentServlet.java and IoUtil.java, which write unvalidated user input into HTTP responses with text/javascript content type.

evidence.notes: The actual XSS vulnerabilities have been filed separately at AgentServlet.java:499 and IoUtil.java:36.

flags.message: System.out.println writes to server stdout, not to an HTTP response - XSS is not exploitable here
flags.evidence: The code `System.out.println(message)` writes to the JVM's standard output stream. XSS requires user-controlled data to be reflected into an HTTP response that is rendered by a browser. Writing to stdout does not satisfy this requirement under any circumstance.

flags.message: CodeQL incorrectly labeled a logging sink as an XSS sink
flags.evidence: The StdoutLogHandler.log() method is clearly a server-side logging utility. The actual XSS sinks in this codebase are reportedly at AgentServlet.java:499 and IoUtil.java:36 where JSONP callbacks are written to HTTP responses.

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a confirmed false positive. The code writes to System.out (server-side standard output), which cannot cause XSS since XSS requires user-controlled data to be reflected into an HTTP response rendered by a browser. CodeQL has misidentified a logging sink as an XSS sink, likely due to taint flow tracking that ends at this print statement without properly classifying the output channel. The finding should be rejected.

findings.description: User-controlled MIME type parameter allows setting response Content-Type to text/html, potentially enabling XSS if response body contains HTML-renderable content
findings.reasoning: The `getMimeType()` method allows the response Content-Type to be set by the user via the `mimeType` request parameter (ConfigKey.MIME_TYPE). An attacker can set `mimeType=text/html` to make the browser render the response as HTML. While the JSON body is serialized (which encodes most special chars), the JSONP callback path combined with this could further enable injection. Additionally, if error messages include unencoded characters, the text/html content type would make the browser attempt to render them. This requires manual review to determine if any JSON response path produces HTML-renderable XSS payloads given the text/html MIME type override.

evidence.notes: The default content type is text/plain which is safe. The JSONP path overrides this with text/javascript. The mimeType parameter risk depends on whether json-simple properly encodes all HTML special chars in all cases.

flags.message: Exploitability depends on response body content and JSON encoding completeness
flags.evidence: The JSON library (json-simple) encodes HTML special characters in string values, making pure JSON responses with text/html generally safe. However, edge cases like numeric values, null, or JSONP callbacks could still be vectors.

flags.message: Content-Type header injection via user-controlled MIME type parameter
flags.evidence: The getMimeType() method at line 384-390 directly passes the user-supplied 'mimeType' request parameter as the Content-Type header value without validation or sanitization. This is a legitimate concern but severity depends on actual rendering behavior.

flags.message: Defense-in-depth concern rather than direct high-severity vulnerability
flags.evidence: The default MIME type is 'text/plain' (safe). JSONP overrides to 'text/javascript'. The user-controlled override to 'text/html' is a design choice that increases attack surface, but actual XSS requires additional conditions to be met (unencoded HTML in response body).

sanity_check.reasoning: The code does allow user-controlled MIME type to be set, which is a legitimate security concern when combined with response body content. However, the actual exploitability is limited because JSON serialization encodes most HTML special characters, and the default/JSONP paths are safe. This is a genuine defense-in-depth issue that warrants review, particularly to verify whether all response paths (including error messages) properly encode HTML characters when text/html content type can be set by an attacker.

findings.description: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into text/javascript response — XSS via JSONP callback injection (AgentServlet streaming path)
findings.reasoning: The `callback` parameter is taken directly from the HTTP request query string (`pReq.getParameter("callback")`) in AgentServlet.java line 472 with no validation, sanitization, or encoding. It is then passed through the call chain to `IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose()` where it is written verbatim into the HTTP response body with `pWriter.write(callback)`. The response Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when a callback is present (AgentServlet.java line 473), which means browsers will execute the response as JavaScript. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a URL with a malicious `callback` parameter value such as `alert(document.cookie)//`. This is a classic JSONP callback injection leading to reflected XSS.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Confirmed via trace_dataflow_path: AgentServlet.java:473 (callback read from request param) -> AgentServlet.java:481 (passed to sendStreamingResponse) -> AgentServlet.java:490,492 (passed to IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose as pCallback) -> IoUtil.java:30 (received as callback param) -> IoUtil.java:36 (written to response via pWriter.write(callback)). No sanitization step exists anywhere in this chain.
evidence.notes: Content-Type is explicitly set to text/javascript when callback is non-null, making this directly exploitable in browsers. No alphanumeric-only check or any other validation exists on the callback value.

message.text: Dataflow path from source to sink

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker tricks a victim into clicking a crafted URL pointing to the Jolokia endpoint with a malicious JSONP callback parameter, causing the victim's browser to execute arbitrary JavaScript with the victim's session context.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must have access to the Jolokia endpoint (could be internal network); attacker needs to craft a URL and trick victim into clicking it
exploit_payload.category: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via JSONP callback injection

findings.description: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into text/javascript response — XSS via JSONP callback injection (AgentServlet non-streaming/sendAllJSON path)
findings.code_snippet: String content = callback == null ? json : callback + "(" + json + ");";
findings.reasoning: In the non-streaming (fallback) code path of `sendAllJSON()`, the raw unvalidated `callback` request parameter is used to construct the response body via string concatenation: `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. This is written directly to the HTTP response output stream as bytes. The Content-Type is `text/javascript` when a callback is present (line 473). There is no encoding, validation, or sanitization of the callback value before it appears in the response body. This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript into the response.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Same source (AgentServlet.java:472, pReq.getParameter("callback")) flows to sendAllJSON at line 485 -> line 499 where callback is directly concatenated into response string.
evidence.notes: This is a second path to the same vulnerability (the non-streaming fallback). Both streaming and non-streaming paths are vulnerable.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker tricks a victim into clicking a crafted URL to the Jolokia endpoint with a malicious callback parameter; the non-streaming response path concatenates the raw callback value into the response body returned as text/javascript, executing in the victim's browser.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must have network access to Jolokia endpoint; attacker needs to socially engineer click on crafted link
exploit_payload.category: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via JSONP callback injection

findings.description: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into text/javascript response — XSS via JSONP callback injection (JolokiaHttpHandler JVM agent, streaming path)
findings.reasoning: In `JolokiaHttpHandler.sendStreamingResponse()`, the JSONP `callback` value is read from the parsed URI query parameter (`pParsedUri.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue())`) at line 346 with no validation or sanitization. It is then passed directly to `IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose()` which writes it verbatim to the response via `pWriter.write(callback)`. The Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when callback is present (line 384 in getMimeType), making the response executable as JavaScript in browsers. This is the JVM agent variant of the same JSONP callback XSS present in AgentServlet.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: callback from pParsedUri.getParameter() (user-controlled) -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() -> pWriter.write(callback) in IoUtil.java:36. No sanitization in chain.
evidence.notes: This is the JVM agent handler (not the servlet agent). Same vulnerability pattern as AgentServlet but in a different deployment context (standalone JVM agent using com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler).

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker tricks a victim into clicking a crafted link to a Jolokia JVM agent endpoint with a malicious JSONP callback parameter, causing JavaScript execution in the victim's browser.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must have network access to Jolokia JVM agent port; attacker needs to socially engineer victim to click crafted URL
exploit_payload.category: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via JSONP callback injection

flags.message: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into text/javascript response — classic JSONP callback injection XSS
flags.evidence: Code confirms: callback = pParsedUri.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue()) with no sanitization, passed to IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() which writes it verbatim. Content-Type is set to text/javascript when callback is present.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly shows an unsanitized JSONP callback parameter read from the HTTP request query string and written verbatim into a text/javascript response. This is a textbook JSONP callback injection / XSS vulnerability — an attacker can craft a malicious callback value containing arbitrary JavaScript (e.g., `alert(1)//`) that will execute in the victim's browser. The code is production runtime code in the JVM agent's HTTP handler, making it directly reachable. The data flow is confirmed: user input -> getParameter() -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() -> pWriter.write(callback) with no validation step in between.

findings.description: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into text/javascript response — XSS via JSONP callback injection (JolokiaHttpHandler JVM agent, non-streaming/sendAllJSON path)
findings.code_snippet: String content = callback == null ? json : callback + "(" + json + ");";
findings.reasoning: In `JolokiaHttpHandler.sendAllJSON()` (non-streaming/fallback path), the JSONP `callback` value is read from the parsed URI query parameters at line 362 (`pParsedUri.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue())`) with no validation or sanitization. It is then directly string-concatenated into the response body: `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. The Content-Type is `text/javascript` when callback is non-null (via `getMimeType()`), so browsers will execute the response as JavaScript. This is the JVM agent non-streaming variant of the JSONP callback XSS vulnerability.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: callback from pParsedUri.getParameter() (user-controlled URL parameter) -> directly concatenated into content string -> written to HTTP response body as text/javascript. No sanitization anywhere in the chain.
evidence.notes: This is the non-streaming (fallback) path in the JVM agent handler. Both streaming and non-streaming paths in both agent types (servlet and JVM) are vulnerable to the same JSONP callback XSS pattern.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker tricks a victim into clicking a crafted link to Jolokia JVM agent endpoint with a malicious JSONP callback parameter; the non-streaming sendAllJSON path directly concatenates the raw callback into the response body returned as text/javascript.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must have network access to Jolokia JVM agent port; attacker needs to socially engineer victim to click crafted URL
exploit_payload.category: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via JSONP callback injection

flags.message: JSONP callback injection XSS in JVM agent HTTP handler
flags.evidence: The callback parameter from the URL query string is directly concatenated into the response body served as text/javascript with no sanitization or validation. This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly shows that the JSONP callback parameter is taken directly from user-controlled URL query parameters and concatenated into the HTTP response body without any sanitization. The response is served with Content-Type text/javascript, meaning browsers will execute the content as JavaScript. This is a classic JSONP callback injection XSS vulnerability in production runtime code of a monitoring/middleware library. The code trace is accurate and the vulnerability is directly exploitable by crafting a URL with a malicious callback parameter value.

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unencoded into HTTP response body as JavaScript function name in JolokiaHttpHandler.sendAllJSON
findings.reasoning: The JSONP `callback` parameter is retrieved directly from the HTTP request URI via `pParsedUri.getParameter()`, with no validation, sanitization, or encoding applied. It is then concatenated directly into the response body as `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. The Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when a callback is present (getMimeType returns "text/javascript"). This allows an attacker to craft a URL like `?callback=alert(1)//` that results in a response body of `alert(1)//({"status":200,...});` with Content-Type text/javascript, executing arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser. No alphanumeric-only validation exists anywhere in the codebase for this parameter.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ParsedUri.java:62 (query string parsed) -> ParsedUri.java:93-102 (getParameter returns value) -> JolokiaHttpHandler.java:362 (assigned to callback) -> JolokiaHttpHandler.java:363 (concatenated into content string) -> JolokiaHttpHandler.java:364 (getBytes()) -> JolokiaHttpHandler.java:367 (written to HTTP response OutputStream)
evidence.notes: Content-Type is set to text/javascript when callback is present, making this directly exploitable as XSS. No X-Content-Type-Options header checked. No alphanumeric validation of callback name.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
GET /jolokia/read/java.lang:type=Memory/HeapMemoryUsage?callback=alert(document.cookie)// HTTP/1.1
Host: target:8778

# Response:
# HTTP/1.1 200 OK
# Content-Type: text/javascript; charset=utf-8
# 
# alert(document.cookie)//({
#   "request": {"type":"read","mbean":"java.lang:type=Memory","attribute":"HeapMemoryUsage"},
#   "value": {...},
#   "status": 200
# });
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with a malicious JSONP callback parameter that is reflected unencoded into a text/javascript response, executing arbitrary JavaScript when the victim visits the crafted URL
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must visit a crafted URL pointing to the Jolokia JVM agent endpoint (no authentication required by default)

flags.message: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into response body as JavaScript
flags.evidence: The callback parameter is taken directly from the URL query string and concatenated into the HTTP response body with Content-Type text/javascript, with no alphanumeric validation, sanitization, or encoding. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via the callback parameter.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly shows that the JSONP callback parameter is read directly from the request URI with no validation or sanitization, then concatenated into the response body which is served as text/javascript. This is a well-documented JSONP XSS vulnerability pattern: any attacker who can trick a user into visiting a crafted URL pointing at a Jolokia endpoint can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser context. The data flow is confirmed by the raw code snippet, and no validation logic exists in the codebase according to the evidence. This is a legitimate high-severity XSS vulnerability in production web-serving code.

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unencoded into HTTP response body as JavaScript function name in AgentServlet.sendAllJSON
findings.reasoning: In AgentServlet.sendAllJSON, the callback parameter obtained from `pReq.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue())` (line 472) is passed directly to this method and concatenated without any encoding or validation into the response body as `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. The Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when a callback is present (line 473: `callback != null ? "text/javascript"`). This is the servlet agent path (WAR/servlet container deployment) version of the same JSONP XSS vulnerability present in JolokiaHttpHandler. No sanitization of the callback parameter exists anywhere in the call chain.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HttpServletRequest.getParameter("callback") -> AgentServlet.sendResponse() line 472 -> sendAllJSON(pResp, callback, pJson) line 486 -> callback + "(" + json + ");" line 500 -> out.write(response) line 503
evidence.notes: This is the servlet-container (WAR deployment) version of the JSONP XSS vulnerability. Content-Type text/javascript is set for JSONP responses, making this directly exploitable.

GET /jolokia/read/java.lang:type=Memory/HeapMemoryUsage?callback=alert(document.cookie)// HTTP/1.1
Host: target/jolokia

# Response:

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with a malicious JSONP callback parameter directed at the Jolokia servlet agent endpoint, which reflects the callback unencoded as a text/javascript response, executing arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must visit crafted URL pointing to Jolokia servlet agent (typically deployed alongside a Java application server)

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unencoded into HTTP response body via streaming path in IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose
findings.reasoning: IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose is the shared streaming implementation called from both AgentServlet.sendStreamingResponse (line 492) and JolokiaHttpHandler.sendStreamingResponse (line 348). The `callback` parameter passed in is written directly to the HTTP response Writer with `pWriter.write(callback)` without any validation or encoding. Since streaming is the default mode (ConfigKey.STREAMING defaults to true), this is the primary code path for JSONP responses. The callback comes from the raw HTTP request parameter with no alphanumeric validation anywhere in the call chain. This is exploitable in the same way as the non-streaming sendAllJSON paths.

evidence.code_context:
IoUtil.java lines 30-45: callback written directly to writer without validation.

Called from AgentServlet.java line 492:
    IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose(writer, pJson, pCallback);
where pCallback = pReq.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue()) (line 472)

Called from JolokiaHttpHandler.java line 348:
    IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose(writer, pJson, callback);
where callback = pParsedUri.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue()) (line 345)

Streaming is default/enabled path per configuration.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request parameter 'callback' -> AgentServlet/JolokiaHttpHandler -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose(writer, pJson, callback) -> pWriter.write(callback) -> HTTP response body
evidence.notes: This is the streaming path version of the same JSONP XSS. Since streaming is the default mode, this is the PRIMARY exploitation path. The callback is written as the first thing to the response writer, directly followed by '(' and the JSON body.

# Response (streaming/chunked):

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted URL with a malicious JSONP callback to the Jolokia streaming endpoint; the callback is written directly to the chunked HTTP response as JavaScript, executing in the victim's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must visit crafted URL pointing to Jolokia agent; streaming mode is default so this is the primary execution path
exploit_payload.category: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (JSONP XSS) - Streaming Path

flags.message: JSONP callback parameter reflected without sanitization in streaming path
flags.evidence: pWriter.write(callback) at line 36 writes the raw HTTP request parameter directly to the response body without any validation, encoding, or sanitization. This allows injection of arbitrary JavaScript via the callback parameter.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly shows the `callback` parameter being written directly to the HTTP response writer without any sanitization or encoding, constituting a classic JSONP-based XSS (CWE-79/CWE-116). The data flow is confirmed: HTTP request parameter -> AgentServlet/JolokiaHttpHandler -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose -> pWriter.write(callback) -> response body. This is production runtime code in a web middleware/monitoring framework, the vulnerability is fully exploitable via the default streaming path, and no defensive measures (allowlist validation, character encoding, regex filtering) are present. This is a legitimate high-severity finding.

findings.description: False positive: LogHandler.java sink writes to stdout (System.out.println), not HTTP response - no XSS possible
findings.reasoning: The CodeQL flows for queries 1 (xss-jsonp-callback) and 3 (xss-request-uri-in-error) both point to LogHandler.java:82 as the sink. However, examining the actual code, the StdoutLogHandler.log() method (line 82) writes to System.out.println() - this is console output, not an HTTP response. The URI/request data in debug log messages (HttpRequestHandler.java:82-83 logs URI via logHandler.debug) flows to stdout logging only, not to any HTTP response body. This is not exploitable for XSS as it requires access to the server console, not a browser context. The real XSS sinks are in sendAllJSON and IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose which have already been reported.

evidence.code_context:
LogHandler.java lines 79-82 (StdoutLogHandler.log):
    private void log(String message) {
        System.out.println(message); //NOSONAR
    }

This is stdout output only. The debug calls in HttpRequestHandler.java:
    logHandler.debug("URI: " + pUri);  // line 82
    logHandler.debug("Path-Info: " + pathInfo);  // line 83
flow to this stdout logger, not to any HTTP response writer or OutputStream connected to the HTTP response.
evidence.notes: CodeQL incorrectly identified the stdout logger as an XSS sink. The actual XSS sinks are sendAllJSON and IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose which have been reported separately.

findings.description: False positive: sendForbidden writes static 403 message to plain text response - SecurityException messages contain mostly server-controlled data
findings.reasoning: The sendForbidden method writes a 403 response with the SecurityException message. While the Origin header (user-controlled) is included in some exception messages ("Origin X is not allowed"), this is not a realistic XSS vector because: (1) No Content-Type is explicitly set on the 403 response, and modern browsers will typically treat it as text/plain based on sniffing; (2) The data is IP addresses, Origin headers, and HTTP method names - not arbitrary HTML; (3) The response is triggered only when access is denied, limiting attacker control. The primary XSS concerns (JSONP callback) have already been reported separately. Query 2 (jolokia-jvm-handler-xss) flagging this as "Response write in JolokiaHttpHandler.sendForbidden - argument: getBytes(...)" is a low-confidence signal that does not represent a true XSS vulnerability.

evidence.code_context:
JolokiaHttpHandler.java lines 318-327: sendForbidden writes static '403 (Forbidden)\n' plus optional SecurityException message. No Content-Type header set.

SecurityException sources that could include user input:
- HttpRequestHandler.java:284: 'No access from client ' + pAddress + ' allowed' (IP address)
- HttpRequestHandler.java:287: 'Origin ' + pOrigin + ' is not allowed' (Origin header value)

However, Origin header values are constrained to URL format and would not typically enable XSS in a plain text response. No explicit text/html Content-Type is set.
evidence.notes: Not a realistic XSS vector. The real JSONP XSS vulnerabilities are in sendAllJSON and IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose.

flags.message: No realistic XSS vector due to lack of HTML content-type and constrained user input format
flags.evidence: No Content-Type: text/html is set on the response. The user-controlled input (Origin header) is constrained to URL format. Modern browsers will sniff this as text/plain. The SecurityException messages embed IP addresses and Origin URLs, not arbitrary HTML/script content.

flags.message: Best practice would be to explicitly set Content-Type: text/plain and sanitize reflected input, but this is not a realistic attack vector
flags.evidence: While the Origin header value is reflected in the error response, XSS via reflected content in a 403 plain-text response without HTML content-type is not practically exploitable in modern browsers.

sanity_check.reasoning: The sendForbidden method reflects SecurityException messages (which may include the Origin header value) into a 403 HTTP response, but this is not a realistic XSS vector. No Content-Type: text/html is set, the input is constrained to URL-format strings, and browsers will treat the response as plain text. The finding correctly identifies the false positive — real XSS risks in this codebase are noted to be in JSONP endpoints (sendAllJSON, IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose), not in this plain-text error handler.

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unvalidated into JavaScript response body (XSS via JSONP callback injection) in AgentServlet
findings.reasoning: The `callback` parameter is taken directly from the HTTP request (`pReq.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue())`) with no validation or sanitization. It is then concatenated verbatim into the response body as a JavaScript expression: `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. The response Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when a callback is present. This means any attacker-controlled value in the `callback` query parameter — including arbitrary JavaScript — will be placed at the start of a `text/javascript` response that browsers will execute. There is no alphanumeric-only check, no regex validation, and no encoding applied to the callback value. This is a classic JSONP callback injection / XSS vulnerability.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Confirmed by trace_dataflow_path: callback flows from pReq.getParameter() at line 472 -> sendAllJSON() at line 485 -> sendAllJSON() parameter at line 495 -> string concatenation at line 499 with no sanitization step.
evidence.notes: No validation exists in the codebase. IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() also writes the callback directly without any encoding.

message.text: Dataflow path from source to sink

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL containing a malicious JSONP callback (e.g., ?callback=alert(document.cookie)//) and tricks a victim into clicking it; the browser executes the JavaScript because the response is Content-Type: text/javascript
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must click a crafted link pointing to the Jolokia agent endpoint; no authentication required if the endpoint is exposed

flags.message: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected into JavaScript response - classic XSS via JSONP callback injection
flags.evidence: Line 472: callback = pReq.getParameter(...) with no validation; Line 499: callback + '(' + json + ');' concatenated directly into text/javascript response

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly demonstrates a textbook JSONP callback injection vulnerability: the callback parameter is read directly from the HTTP request without any validation, sanitization, or allowlist checking, then concatenated verbatim into a text/javascript response. An attacker can craft a URL with an arbitrary JavaScript payload in the callback parameter (e.g., `callback=alert(document.cookie)//`) that will be executed in the victim's browser. This is production middleware/monitoring agent code with external HTTP input, making it fully exploitable. The dataflow trace is accurate and confirmed by the raw code snippet.

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unvalidated into JavaScript response body (XSS via JSONP callback injection) in JolokiaHttpHandler
findings.code_snippet:
String callback = pParsedUri.getParameter(ConfigKey.CALLBACK.getKeyValue());
String content = callback == null ? json : callback + "(" + json + ");";
findings.reasoning: In JolokiaHttpHandler's `sendAllJSON` method, the `callback` parameter is extracted from the parsed URI without any validation and directly concatenated into the response body as JavaScript: `callback + "(" + json + ");"`. The Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when a callback parameter is present (as determined by `getMimeType()`). There is no alphanumeric-only validation, no regex check, and no encoding of the callback value. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript into the response body by crafting a request with a malicious `callback` parameter value. This is the JVM agent variant of the same JSONP callback injection XSS present in AgentServlet.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: callback flows from pParsedUri.getParameter() at line 362 directly into string concatenation at line 364 (callback + '(' + json + ');') with no sanitization. Content-Type is text/javascript when callback is set (line 383).
evidence.notes: Same pattern as AgentServlet.java. The streaming variant (sendStreamingResponse at line 345) also passes the unvalidated callback to IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose which writes it directly with pWriter.write(callback).

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL targeting the Jolokia JVM agent HTTP handler with a malicious JSONP callback parameter; when a victim visits the crafted URL, the browser executes the injected JavaScript in the context of the Jolokia agent origin
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must click a crafted link to the JVM agent endpoint; Jolokia JVM agent must be running and accessible

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unvalidated into JavaScript streaming response (XSS via JSONP callback injection) in JolokiaHttpHandler.sendStreamingResponse
findings.reasoning: In JolokiaHttpHandler's `sendStreamingResponse` method, the `callback` parameter is extracted from the parsed URI without any validation and passed directly to `IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose()`. Inside that method, the callback is written verbatim to the response writer: `pWriter.write(callback); pWriter.write("("); pJson.writeJSONString(pWriter); pWriter.write(");");`. The Content-Type is `text/javascript` when a callback parameter is present. There is no validation or encoding of the callback value, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript via the `callback` URL parameter.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: callback flows from pParsedUri.getParameter() at line 345 -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() at line 348 -> pWriter.write(callback) in IoUtil.java with no sanitization.
evidence.notes: This is the streaming variant of the same JSONP injection. Both sendStreamingResponse and sendAllJSON in JolokiaHttpHandler.java are vulnerable.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a request to Jolokia JVM agent with malicious callback parameter; the streaming response writes the unvalidated callback directly to the response body as text/javascript, causing the browser to execute the injected script
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must access a crafted URL targeting the JVM agent; Jolokia JVM agent must be running and accessible

flags.message: Unvalidated JSONP callback parameter reflected directly into JavaScript response (XSS via JSONP injection)
flags.evidence: The callback parameter is read from the URI query string at line 345 and passed without any sanitization to IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose(), where it is written verbatim before the JSON payload: pWriter.write(callback); pWriter.write("("); pJson.writeJSONString(pWriter); pWriter.write(");"); with Content-Type text/javascript. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via the callback parameter.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code trace is accurate and well-supported by both the raw file snippet and the IoUtil evidence. The callback parameter flows directly from user-controlled URI input to the response body as part of a JSONP wrapper with no validation, sanitization, or allowlist check. Since this is a library/framework with a web/middleware layer (Jolokia JVM agent), the HTTP endpoint is externally reachable, making this a genuinely exploitable reflected XSS vulnerability in JSONP mode. The Content-Type of text/javascript ensures browsers will execute injected script.

findings.description: JSONP callback parameter reflected unvalidated into JavaScript streaming response (XSS via JSONP callback injection) in AgentServlet.sendStreamingResponse
findings.reasoning: In AgentServlet's `sendStreamingResponse` method, the `pCallback` parameter (sourced unvalidated from the HTTP request at line 472) is passed directly to `IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose()`, which writes it verbatim to the response: `pWriter.write(callback)`. The Content-Type is set to `text/javascript` when callback is non-null (line 473). No validation or encoding is applied. This is the streaming variant of the JSONP injection in AgentServlet, complementing the `sendAllJSON` variant at line 499.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: callback flows from pReq.getParameter() at line 472 -> sendStreamingResponse() at line 481 -> IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() at line 492 -> pWriter.write(callback) in IoUtil.java with no sanitization step.
evidence.notes: This is the streaming code path. The non-streaming path (sendAllJSON) was already reported separately. Both paths share the same unvalidated callback source.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a URL with a malicious JSONP callback parameter targeting the Jolokia servlet endpoint with streaming enabled; the browser receives and executes the injected JavaScript from the text/javascript streaming response
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim must click a crafted link to the Jolokia servlet endpoint; streaming must be enabled (which is the preferred code path)

flags.message: JSONP callback injection via unvalidated user input reflected into text/javascript response
flags.evidence: The callback parameter from pReq.getParameter() flows directly to IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose() which writes it verbatim. Content-Type is set to text/javascript. No sanitization or validation is applied to the callback value before writing.

sanity_check.reasoning: The vulnerability is clearly present and accurately described. The `sendStreamingResponse` method accepts a `pCallback` parameter derived directly from an HTTP request parameter without any validation or encoding, and passes it to `IoUtil.streamResponseAndClose()` which writes it into a `text/javascript` response. This is a classic JSONP callback injection / XSS vulnerability where an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by manipulating the callback parameter. The code is production runtime code in a web-facing servlet (AgentServlet), making it directly exploitable by external users who can craft malicious callback values.
